programs.name: AHCCCS for Elders
programs.tagline: Free long-term care services for Arizona seniors who need daily help with bathing, dressing, or medications.

localEntities.name: Local DES/Family Assistance Administration offices statewide

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ in Arizona and needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management, AHCCCS for Elders can cover nursing home care, in-home personal care, adult day programs, and assisted living. Because this program requires proving your parent needs nursing home level of care (not just low income), you'll need medical documentation showing they need substantial daily assistance.

The program covers all long-term care services through Arizona's Medicaid system. Your parent may need to pay a monthly "share of cost" (like a deductible) if their income exceeds certain limits, but the program covers the remaining costs. Income limits: $2,982/month for a single person, approximately $5,964/month for couples (2026).

Because slots for home-based care have waitlists in many areas, nursing facility placement often moves faster than getting services at home. Wait times vary significantly by region and are managed by different health plans across Arizona.

summary: Needs nursing home level of care

exemptAssets: Life insurance up to $1,500 face value

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need nursing home level of care (daily assistance with bathing, dressing, medication management, or mobility)
otherRequirements: Arizona resident
U.S. citizen or qualified immigrant
Social Security Number

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at Health-e-Arizona Plus or call (888) 621-6880; processing typically takes weeks to months including care assessment.

steps.title: Gather financial documents
steps.description: Collect 3 months of bank statements, income proof, and asset documentation. The $2,000 asset limit is strictly enforced.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Apply online at Health-e-Arizona Plus portal or call ALTCS at (888) 621-6880. You'll need form DE-828.

steps.title: Complete care assessment
steps.description: AHCCCS will schedule an assessment to determine if your parent meets nursing home level of care. This requires medical documentation.

steps.title: Wait for approval
steps.description: Financial review and care assessment take weeks to months. Nursing facility placement typically moves faster than home-based services.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Weeks to months for complete review including care assessment
applicationGuide.waitlist: Possible for home and community-based services; nursing facility may have shorter waits
applicationGuide.tip: Start gathering medical records early—the care assessment requires detailed documentation of daily assistance needs.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Cash value of life insurance over $1,500

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Proof of Arizona residency (utility bill, lease)

categories.name: Medical Documentation
items: Recent medical records showing care needs
Documentation of assistance with daily activities

contentSections.text: Home-based services often have waitlists due to limited slots. Nursing facility placement typically moves faster if your parent needs immediate care.
Arizona recovers costs from your parent's estate after death for services received after age 55. This affects inheritance planning but doesn't prevent qualification.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies?
faqs.answer: Yes, if she intends to return home and the home equity is under $713,000. The house doesn't count toward the $2,000 asset limit. However, Arizona may recover costs from the home's sale after her death.

faqs.question: What if her income is just over the limit?
faqs.answer: She may still qualify but will pay a monthly "share of cost" (like a deductible) equal to the amount over the income limit. AHCCCS covers remaining costs after she meets her share of cost each month.

faqs.question: How do they determine if she needs nursing home level of care?
faqs.answer: AHCCCS conducts an assessment looking at her ability to safely perform daily activities like bathing, dressing, medication management, and mobility. You'll need medical records documenting these limitations.

faqs.question: Can she get services at home instead of a nursing facility?
faqs.answer: Yes, but home and community-based services often have waitlists. Services can include personal care, adult day programs, home-delivered meals, and respite care, but availability varies by region.

faqs.question: What happens if she has Medicare too?
faqs.answer: She can have both Medicare and AHCCCS. Medicare covers medical care while AHCCCS covers long-term care services that Medicare doesn't pay for, like ongoing personal care assistance.

faqs.question: How long does the application process take?
faqs.answer: Typically weeks to months because it includes both financial review and clinical assessment. The process can't start before she establishes Arizona residency, and wait times vary by region and contractor.

programs.name: Arizona Long Term Care System (ALTCS)
programs.tagline: Arizona's Medicaid program that covers long-term care costs when your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or other basic activities.
programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ (or disabled) and needs daily help with bathing, dressing, eating, or other basic activities, ALTCS can cover the full cost of their long-term care — whether that's in-home care, assisted living, memory care, or nursing facility care. Because ALTCS is Arizona's Medicaid long-term care program, it covers services that regular Medicare doesn't, but your parent must meet strict income and asset limits.

Your parent can qualify with income up to $2,901/month for a single person (2025) and assets below $2,000. Unlike many programs, ALTCS covers care wherever your parent lives — they don't need to move to a nursing facility first. The program requires both financial eligibility and a clinical assessment proving they need help with at least two activities of daily living.

exemptAssets: Primary residence in Arizona (up to $752,000 home equity)

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need assistance with at least two activities of daily living (bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, or continence) or require nursing facility level of care
otherRequirements: Arizona resident
U.S. citizen or proper immigration status

applicationGuide.summary: Call (888) 621-6880 to request application forms — processing takes 60-90 days.

steps.title: Call ALTCS
steps.description: Call (888) 621-6880 to request application forms and instructions. A family member or caregiver can call on your parent's behalf.

steps.title: Complete Form DE-828
steps.description: Fill out the ALTCS Application completely — any mistakes result in automatic denial.

steps.title: Gather documentation
steps.description: Collect proof of income, assets, residency, and physician certification of long-term care needs.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Submit completed application with all required documents for financial and medical review.

applicationGuide.tip: Complete the application with zero errors — any mistakes result in automatic denial and you'll need to start over.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
excluded: Primary residence in Arizona (up to $752,000 home equity)
Special Needs Trusts (for disabled persons under 65)

categories.name: Identity
items: Proof of citizenship or immigration status

categories.name: Medical
items: Physician's certification of need for long-term care
Documentation of activities of daily living limitations

contentSections.text: 60-month look-back rule: If your parent gave away assets or sold them below fair market value in the past 5 years, they may face a penalty period of ineligibility.
A dementia or Alzheimer's diagnosis alone does not guarantee eligibility — your parent must still demonstrate they need help with at least two daily activities.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for ALTCS?
faqs.answer: Yes, your parent can keep their primary Arizona residence as long as the home equity doesn't exceed $752,000 (2026 limit). The house is exempt from the $2,000 asset limit.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is just over the $2,901 limit?
faqs.answer: If his income exceeds $2,901/month, he won't qualify for ALTCS. The income limits are strict and updated annually in January. Consider consulting with a Medicaid planning attorney about spend-down strategies.

faqs.question: Does Mom need to be in a nursing home already to apply?
faqs.answer: No, your parent does not need to already live in a nursing facility to qualify. ALTCS covers care in your parent's own home, assisted living, memory care, or nursing facilities based on their needs.

faqs.question: What happens if we make a mistake on the application?
faqs.answer: Any mistakes on the application result in automatic denial. You would need to start the entire 60-90 day process over. Consider working with a lawyer or Medicaid planning service to ensure accuracy.

faqs.question: Can I apply for ALTCS on behalf of my parent?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members, caregivers, or estate planning lawyers can assist with or submit the application on your parent's behalf. Call (888) 621-6880 to get started.

faqs.question: What if my parents are married and only one needs long-term care?
faqs.answer: Married couples have different asset limits ($4,000 vs. $2,000 for singles) and special community spouse protections. The healthy spouse may be able to keep more assets and income — this is determined case-by-case.

programs.name: Arizona PACE Programs
programs.tagline: All-inclusive medical care for parents 55+ who need nursing home-level care but want to stay home.

localEntities.name: InnovAge operates PACE/LIFE centers in Arizona (among other states: California, Colorado, Florida, New Mexico, Pennsylvania, Virginia)
Specific PACE organizations and their service areas not detailed in search results

programs.intro:
If your parent is 55+ and needs significant daily help but wants to avoid a nursing home, Arizona PACE provides all medical care, medications, and social services through one coordinated team. Your parent will never pay deductibles or co-pays for any PACE-covered care. Because PACE covers everything from doctor visits to transportation to adult day programs under one plan, you won't need to coordinate between separate Medicare providers — one interdisciplinary team manages all care.

PACE requires your parent to qualify for nursing home-level care (daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management), but the program is specifically designed to keep them living safely in the community. About 90% of participants have their care fully covered through Medicaid. The rest pay private monthly premiums directly to the PACE organization.

PACE is not available statewide. Your parent must live in an area where a PACE organization operates, and availability varies significantly by region in Arizona.

summary: Must live in PACE service area
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be certified as needing nursing home-level care (daily assistance with activities like bathing, dressing, medication management, or mobility)
otherRequirements: Must live in PACE organization service area
Must be able to live safely in community with PACE services
Cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage or Medicare prescription drug plans
Cannot be receiving hospice services

applicationGuide.summary: Call your regional AHCCCS office to start the application process — processing time varies by region.

steps.title: Call your regional AHCCCS office
steps.description: Central Arizona: 800-564-5465, Northern Arizona: 888-788-4408, Southern Arizona: 866-495-6738

steps.title: Verify PACE availability
steps.description: Confirm a PACE organization operates in your parent's specific area — not all Arizona counties have PACE programs

steps.title: Complete AHCCCS application
steps.description: Submit income and medical documentation to establish Medicaid eligibility (if seeking Medicaid funding)

steps.title: Schedule PACE intake assessment
steps.description: PACE organization staff will assess whether your parent meets nursing home level of care requirements and can live safely in community

applicationGuide.tip: Before applying, verify that a PACE organization actually serves your parent's address — many Arizona areas have no PACE coverage.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts (Medicaid Funding Only)
excluded: Primary home (no equity limit in Arizona)

offices.name: Central Arizona Regional Office
offices.address: Covers Maricopa County and surrounding areas

offices.name: Southern Arizona Regional Office
offices.address: Covers Pima County and southern Arizona

categories.name: Medical
items: Recent medical records showing need for daily care assistance

categories.name: Financial (if applying for Medicaid funding)
items: Property tax records for real estate

categories.name: Identity
items: Proof of residence in PACE service area

contentSections.text: PACE is not available statewide. Many Arizona counties have no PACE organizations operating. You must verify coverage in your parent's specific address before applying.
If your parent has Medicare Advantage, they must disenroll before joining PACE. PACE cannot be combined with Medicare Advantage plans.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is just over the Medicaid limit?
faqs.answer: PACE has no income limits itself. If your parent's income exceeds $2,982/month for Medicaid eligibility, they can still join PACE by paying private monthly premiums directly to the PACE organization. About 10% of PACE participants pay privately rather than through Medicaid.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies?
faqs.answer: Yes. PACE is designed to help people stay in their homes rather than move to nursing homes. Arizona has no home equity limit for Medicaid, so your parent can keep their primary residence regardless of value.

faqs.question: What happens if we move to a different Arizona county?
faqs.answer: Your parent can only receive PACE services within their PACE organization's service area. If you move to an area without PACE coverage, they would need to disenroll and transition back to regular Medicare and other services.

faqs.question: How is this different from Medicare Advantage?
faqs.answer: PACE covers all Medicare and Medicaid services plus additional long-term care services like adult day programs and care coordination. Unlike Medicare Advantage, PACE participants never pay deductibles or co-pays for any covered services, and care is managed by one interdisciplinary team.

faqs.question: What if Dad doesn't want to go to the adult day center?
faqs.answer: PACE requires participation in the adult day health center as part of the care plan, typically several days per week. The interdisciplinary team determines the appropriate level of participation based on your parent's health and social needs. This is not optional.

faqs.question: Can we choose our own doctors under PACE?
faqs.answer: No. Once enrolled in PACE, your parent must receive all covered medical care through the PACE organization's team of providers. They cannot see outside specialists or use other medical providers for covered services.

programs.name: Arizona Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
programs.tagline: Arizona covers your parent's Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copays if their income is under $1,800/month.

localEntities.name: Local DES/Family Assistance Administration offices statewide

programs.intro:
If your parent struggles to pay Medicare premiums and medical bills, Arizona's Medicare Savings Programs can eliminate these costs entirely. The state pays Medicare Part B premiums ($174.70/month in 2025), deductibles, coinsurance, and copays directly to providers — your parent never sees these bills. Three tiers exist based on income: QMB covers everything, while SLMB and QI cover premiums plus prescription drug help.

Unlike most states, Arizona has no asset limits — your parent can own their home, have savings, and still qualify if their monthly income falls under the limits (roughly $1,330-$1,800 for a single person depending on the tier). Because providers cannot bill QMB recipients for Medicare-covered services, your parent is protected from surprise medical bills.
programs.savingsSource: Medicare Part B premiums plus typical deductibles and copays

otherRequirements: Must be enrolled in or eligible for Medicare Part A
U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen
Apply for other available pensions/disability benefits

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at Health-e-Arizona or visit your local DES office — processing takes 45 days.

steps.title: Choose your application method
steps.description: Apply online at Health-e-Arizona (fastest) or get a paper application from your local DES office.

steps.title: Gather income documents
steps.description: Collect Social Security statements, pension letters, and bank statements from the last 3 months.

steps.title: Submit with Medicare information
steps.description: Include your parent's Medicare card and proof of Arizona residency (utility bill).

steps.title: Follow up if applying for QI
steps.description: QI funding is limited and first-come, first-served — call AHCCCS at 1-855-432-7587 to check status.

applicationGuide.waitlist: QI program may have waitlist if annual funding runs out; QMB and SLMB have no waitlist
applicationGuide.tip: Apply for the highest tier (QMB) even if you think income might be too high — the $20 monthly disregard and Arizona's no-asset-test policy help many families qualify.

tiers.name: QMB (Qualified Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.coverage: Pays Part A premium (if applicable), Part B premium, all deductibles, coinsurance, and copays. Automatic Extra Help for prescription drugs.

tiers.name: SLMB (Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.coverage: Pays Part B premium only. Automatic Extra Help for prescription drugs.

tiers.name: QI (Qualified Individual)
tiers.coverage: Pays Part B premium only. Automatic Extra Help for prescription drugs. Must reapply annually.

categories.name: Identity
items: Birth certificate or proof of citizenship

contentSections.text: Arizona has no asset limits for Medicare Savings Programs — your parent can own their home, have savings in the bank, and still qualify based on income alone. This makes Arizona more generous than most states.
QI program funding is limited and awarded first-come, first-served. If your parent's income falls in the QI range, apply early in the year and consider reapplying for SLMB as backup.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house and savings if she qualifies?
faqs.answer: Yes. Arizona has no asset limits for Medicare Savings Programs. Your mom can own her home, have money in savings accounts, and still qualify based on income alone.

faqs.question: What if her income is just over the QMB limit?
faqs.answer: She may still qualify for SLMB (pays Part B premium) if income is under $1,596/month for a single person, or QI if under $1,796/month. Each tier provides different levels of help.

faqs.question: Will this affect her Medicare Advantage plan?
faqs.answer: No, she can keep her Medicare Advantage plan. The state will pay her Part B premium and she'll get automatic Extra Help for prescription drugs, which coordinates with her plan.

faqs.question: What happens if she gets denied but her income changes?
faqs.answer: She can reapply anytime her income drops. For QMB and SLMB, there's no waitlist. Report income changes to maintain eligibility or qualify for a higher tier.

faqs.question: Does this count as Medicaid for her other benefits?
faqs.answer: These are Medicare cost-sharing programs, not full Medicaid. They won't affect Social Security, pensions, or other benefits. They only help pay Medicare-related costs.

faqs.question: Can providers still bill her for Medicare services?
faqs.answer: No. If she has QMB, providers cannot bill her for any Medicare-covered services — the state pays everything. SLMB and QI recipients may still have some copays for medical services.

programs.name: Nutrition Assistance (SNAP)
programs.tagline: Up to $234/month in grocery money if your parent is 60+ and on a fixed income.

localEntities.name: Local Family Assistance Administration (FAA) offices via 1-855-777-8590

programs.intro:
If your parent is struggling to afford groceries on Social Security or a pension, SNAP provides monthly money loaded onto an EBT card they can use at grocery stores or online at Amazon and Walmart. Because Arizona has no asset limits for most households, your parent won't lose benefits for having savings or owning their home.

The amount varies by income and household size — a single person can receive up to $234/month, while a two-person household can get up to $546/month. The program calculates benefits as the maximum amount minus 30% of your parent's net income after deductions for medical expenses over $35/month and high shelter costs.

Parents age 60+ get special treatment: no work requirements, higher income limits, and medical expense deductions that can significantly lower their countable income.
programs.savingsSource: Monthly EBT benefits range from $100-234 for individuals, up to $546 for couples

otherRequirements: Must live in Arizona
Social Security, pensions, and disability payments count as income
Asset limits only apply if income is over the gross limit

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at arizonaselfhelp.org or call 1-855-777-8590 — typically processed within 30 days.

steps.title: Screen for eligibility
steps.description: Use the online screener at arizonaselfhelp.org to see if your parent likely qualifies before starting the full application.

steps.title: Gather income documents
steps.description: Collect Social Security award letters, pension statements, and any other income proof for the past month.

steps.title: Document medical expenses
steps.description: Gather bills for medical expenses over $35/month — these reduce countable income and can increase benefits.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Complete the application online at arizonaselfhelp.org or call 1-855-777-8590 to apply by phone.

steps.title: Attend interview
steps.description: Complete a phone or in-person eligibility interview with a caseworker to verify information.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Typically 30 days, expedited processing available for urgent cases
applicationGuide.tip: Medical expenses over $35/month are deductible and can significantly increase your parent's benefits — include everything from Medicare premiums to prescription costs.

contentSections.heading: Gross Income Limits by Household Size (2025-2026)
contentSections.footnote: 185% Federal Poverty Level, Oct 2025 - Sept 2026. If over these limits, may still qualify via net income test.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts (Only if Income Over Limit)
excluded: Life insurance cash value (varies by policy)

offices.name: Statewide Application Line
offices.address: Family Assistance Administration offices throughout Arizona

categories.name: Identity & Residence
items: Proof of Arizona residency (utility bill, lease)

contentSections.text: Arizona has no asset limits for most households — your parent can own their home, have savings, and still qualify. Asset limits ($4,500) only apply to elderly households whose income is over the gross limit but trying to qualify via the net income test.

faqs.question: What if Mom's Social Security is just over the income limit?
faqs.answer: She can still qualify through the net income test. After deductions for medical expenses over $35/month and high shelter costs, her countable income may fall below $1,305/month for a single person. Arizona also has no asset limits for most applicants, so savings won't disqualify her.

faqs.question: Can Dad keep his house and car if he gets SNAP?
faqs.answer: Yes. Arizona exempts all vehicles and the primary home regardless of value. These assets don't count toward any limits. The $4,500 asset limit only applies to bank accounts and investments, and only for households over the gross income limit.

faqs.question: Do Mom's Medicare premiums help her qualify?
faqs.answer: Yes. Medicare Part B and Part D premiums, plus any medical expenses over $35/month, are deducted from her income before calculating SNAP benefits. This can significantly increase her monthly benefit amount.

faqs.question: Will SNAP benefits affect Dad's other programs?
faqs.answer: No. SNAP doesn't affect Social Security, Medicare, Medicaid, or housing assistance. The only conflict is with FDPIR (Food Distribution Program on Indian Reservations) — he can't get both simultaneously.

faqs.question: What if I live with Mom but buy groceries separately?
faqs.answer: If you're 22+ and buy/prepare food separately from your parent, you can apply as separate households. But if you share meals or grocery shopping, everyone in the household must be included in the application, which may affect eligibility.

faqs.question: Can Mom use SNAP for grocery delivery?
faqs.answer: Yes. The EBT card works for online grocery orders through Amazon, Walmart, and Instacart/Aldi. This is especially helpful if your parent has mobility issues or lives in a rural area with limited grocery access.

programs.name: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)
programs.tagline: If your parent struggles with high utility bills, they may qualify for up to $640/year toward electricity or gas costs.

localEntities.name: Local Community Action Programs; contact via (866) 494-1981.

programs.intro:
If your parent is on a fixed income and struggling to pay utility bills, LIHEAP can provide up to $640/year toward heating or cooling costs. The program gives priority to households with someone 60 or older, meaning your parent may move ahead of other applicants.

LIHEAP covers both regular energy assistance (minimum $160, maximum $640 annually) and crisis help (up to $500 for energy emergencies like shut-off notices). Because funding is limited and distributed first-come-first-served within priority groups, applying early in the season is crucial.

The program runs seasonally: heating assistance from December through March, cooling assistance from April through October or November (depending on county). Your parent cannot have a utility credit of $500 or more to qualify.
programs.savingsSource: Standard LIHEAP benefit range, with additional crisis assistance up to $500

otherRequirements: U.S. citizenship or qualifying immigration status
Arizona residency with proof
No utility bill credit of $500 or more
Income verification for all household members 18+ for last 30 days only

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at des.az.gov/liheap or call (866) 494-1981 — processing time not specified but funding runs out.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect ID, proof of residence, Social Security numbers for all household members, and income verification for the last 30 days for adults 18+

steps.title: Apply during seasonal window
steps.description: Heating assistance: December 1 – March 31. Cooling assistance: April 1 – October/November 30 (varies by county)

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Apply online at des.az.gov/liheap, call (866) 494-1981, or schedule appointment with local Community Action Program

steps.title: Wait for priority processing
steps.description: Applications processed by priority: elderly 60+, disabled, veterans, and families with children under 6 go first

applicationGuide.waitlist: Funding may run out — some agencies stop accepting applications early in the season
applicationGuide.tip: Apply as early as possible when the seasonal window opens, especially if your parent is in a priority group (age 60+).

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Household Size
contentSections.footnote: 2026 limits; household includes everyone living at the address who shares the utility bill

tiers.name: Priority 1
tiers.description: Households with elderly (60+), disabled members, veterans, or children under 6
tiers.coverage: Applications processed first within funding limits

tiers.name: General
tiers.coverage: Applications processed after Priority 1 if funding remains

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Proof of citizenship or immigration status
Proof of residence (lease, deed, property tax bill)
Social Security numbers or birth certificates for all household members

categories.name: Financial
items: Income verification for last 30 days for all adults 18+ (paystubs, Social Security award letters, etc.)

contentSections.text: Funding is limited and runs out during the season. Some local agencies stop accepting applications early when their allocation is exhausted. Apply as soon as the seasonal window opens.
Your parent gets priority processing if they're 60 or older, but they still need to apply during the correct season and before funding runs out in their area.

faqs.question: What if Mom's Social Security puts her just over the income limit?
faqs.answer: The income limits are based on gross monthly income for the last 30 days only. If her income varies month to month, the 30-day snapshot used for the application might qualify her even if her annual income seems too high.

faqs.question: Can Dad get both heating and cooling assistance in the same year?
faqs.answer: Yes, these are separate seasonal programs. He can apply for heating assistance (December-March) and cooling assistance (April-October/November) in the same year, up to $640 for each.

faqs.question: What counts as a household for the income limits?
faqs.answer: Everyone living at the same address who shares the utility bill counts as the household, even if they're not related. If your parent lives alone but has a boarder who contributes to utilities, both incomes count.

faqs.question: Will this affect Mom's other benefits like Medicare or Social Security?
faqs.answer: No, LIHEAP is not counted as income for other federal programs. It won't affect her Medicare, Social Security, SNAP, or other benefits.

faqs.question: What if the utility company already shut off Dad's power?
faqs.answer: He may qualify for crisis assistance up to $500 to help with reconnection, in addition to the regular LIHEAP benefit. Apply immediately and mention the shut-off when calling.

faqs.question: Why does the application ask about a $500 utility credit?
faqs.answer: If your parent has a utility bill credit of $500 or more, they're not eligible. This prevents double-dipping from other energy assistance programs.

programs.name: Arizona State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP)
programs.tagline: Free expert help navigating Medicare options and finding programs that could save your parent thousands on healthcare costs.

localEntities.name: Arizona Department of Economic Security (state office, Phoenix)

programs.intro:
If your parent has Medicare, Arizona SHIP provides free one-on-one counseling to help them understand their options and apply for money-saving programs. Because Medicare has dozens of plan choices and supplemental programs, having an expert guide can save your parent thousands — like helping them qualify for Extra Help (prescription drug savings) or Medicare Savings Programs (up to $8,400/year for individuals).

SHIP counselors help with Medicare Advantage vs. Medigap decisions, Part D enrollment, appeals when claims are denied, and applications for financial assistance programs. They also provide fraud protection education through the Senior Medicare Patrol. No income limits apply — the service is free for all Medicare beneficiaries, their families, and caregivers, though they prioritize help for those with limited incomes or disabilities.

otherRequirements: Must be a Medicare beneficiary, family member, or caregiver in Arizona

applicationGuide.summary: Call 800-432-4040 or complete the online form at azship.org — counselors respond promptly to schedule appointments.

steps.title: Contact SHIP
steps.description: Call 800-432-4040 or fill out the intake form at azship.org with your parent's basic information and what help you need.

steps.title: Schedule counseling
steps.description: A local counselor will call back to schedule a phone, in-person, or virtual appointment based on your preference.

steps.title: Prepare documents
steps.description: Gather your parent's Medicare card, current insurance information, and income details if applying for assistance programs.

steps.title: Get personalized help
steps.description: Meet with the counselor who will explain options, help compare plans, and assist with applications for savings programs.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Counselors return calls or schedule appointments promptly after contact
applicationGuide.tip: Even if you think your parent's income is too high for assistance programs, have SHIP check — income limits change yearly and there may be programs you don't know about.

offices.name: Arizona Department of Economic Security
offices.address: 1789 West Jefferson, Site Code MD6288, Phoenix, AZ 85007

contentSections.text: SHIP provides counseling only — they don't pay for healthcare or provide insurance. But they help you apply for programs that do, like Medicare Savings Programs worth up to $8,400/year.

faqs.question: What if my parent already has Medicare — can SHIP still help?
faqs.answer: Yes. SHIP helps people who already have Medicare compare plans during Open Enrollment, apply for Extra Help with prescription costs, appeal denied claims, and find additional savings programs they may have missed.

faqs.question: Does my parent's income matter for getting SHIP counseling?
faqs.answer: No. SHIP is free for all Medicare beneficiaries regardless of income. They prioritize help for people with limited incomes or disabilities, but everyone gets service.

faqs.question: Can I call SHIP as the adult child, or does my parent have to call?
faqs.answer: You can call. SHIP helps family members and caregivers, not just the Medicare beneficiary directly. They understand that adult children often manage their parents' healthcare decisions.

faqs.question: What's the difference between SHIP and the Medicare.gov website?
faqs.answer: SHIP provides personalized one-on-one help from a real person who knows Arizona's specific options. Because Medicare has hundreds of plan combinations, having someone walk through your parent's specific situation is much more useful than trying to figure it out online.

faqs.question: How long does a SHIP counseling session take?
faqs.answer: Sessions vary based on what you need help with. A simple plan comparison might take 30 minutes, while helping with multiple applications for assistance programs could take an hour or more. There's no time limit — they work until your questions are answered.

faqs.question: What if my parent needs help with Medicare fraud or scams?
faqs.answer: SHIP includes the Senior Medicare Patrol program specifically for fraud prevention and reporting. They can help if your parent received suspicious Medicare calls, bills for services they didn't receive, or thinks their Medicare number was stolen.

programs.name: Home Delivered Meals (Meals on Wheels)
programs.tagline: If your parent is homebound or lacks reliable transportation, they may qualify for weekly meal deliveries at no cost.

localEntities.name: YWCA Metropolitan Phoenix (Glendale location: 8561 N 61st Ave)[6]
St. Mary's Food Bank (statewide home delivery)[2]
Mom's Meals (available through Medicaid/Medicare Advantage plans)[3][5]

programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and homebound due to illness, disability, or lack of transportation, they may qualify for free meal deliveries directly to their home. Arizona's home-delivered meals program operates through multiple providers, with benefits ranging from one meal per week through St. Mary's Food Bank to up to seven meals weekly through the YWCA program in Glendale.

Because this program serves homebound seniors who cannot shop or prepare meals independently, it provides both nutrition support and safety through regular wellness checks during delivery. Your parent must be present to receive deliveries, and most programs require household income at or below 185% of the Federal Poverty Level ($2,510/month for a single person in 2026).

The program operates statewide, but your parent's specific benefits depend on which provider serves their area and their referral source. Seniors with Medicaid or Medicare Advantage should check with their health plan first, as meal benefits may be covered at no cost through their insurance.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be confined to home due to physical/mental disability, prolonged illness, COVID-19 risk, or lack reliable transportation (excluding public transit)
otherRequirements: Valid photo ID required at delivery
Must certify household income eligibility

applicationGuide.summary: Call the Area Agency on Aging at 602-264-4357 to determine eligibility and get referred to the appropriate provider.

steps.title: Call Area Agency on Aging
steps.description: Contact 602-264-4357 to discuss your parent's situation and determine which provider serves their area

steps.title: Complete eligibility screening
steps.description: Provide information about income, living situation, and why your parent is homebound

steps.title: Get provider referral
steps.description: AAA will refer your parent to St. Mary's Food Bank, YWCA, or another local provider based on their location

steps.title: Contact health plan if applicable
steps.description: If your parent has Medicaid or Medicare Advantage, call their health plan to ask about meal benefits coverage

applicationGuide.tip: If your parent has Medicaid or Medicare Advantage, call their health plan first—meal benefits may be covered at no cost through their insurance.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Household Size (185% Federal Poverty Level)

offices.name: YWCA Metropolitan Phoenix
offices.address: 8561 N 61st Ave, Glendale (serves Glendale and surrounding areas)

categories.name: Identity
items: Valid photo ID (required at time of delivery)

categories.name: Financial
items: Proof of household income (to verify 185% Federal Poverty Level eligibility)
Medicaid or Medicare Advantage plan information if applicable

categories.name: Medical
items: Documentation of disability or medical condition that keeps your parent homebound

contentSections.text: Your parent must be physically present to receive delivery—meals cannot be left on doorsteps or porches. Delivery windows are typically 6:00 AM to 6:00 PM.

Benefits vary dramatically by provider: St. Mary's Food Bank typically provides one meal per week, while YWCA in Glendale provides up to seven meals weekly. The Area Agency on Aging determines which provider your parent will be referred to based on their location.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is just over the 185% poverty limit?
faqs.answer: If your parent has Medicaid or Medicare Advantage, contact their health plan directly—meal benefits may be available through their insurance regardless of the food bank income limits. Different funding sources have different eligibility rules.

faqs.question: Can my parent get meals if they can drive but just don't want to go grocery shopping?
faqs.answer: No. Your parent must be confined to home due to physical/mental disability, prolonged illness, COVID-19 risk, or lack of reliable transportation. Simply preferring not to shop doesn't qualify.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent isn't home when meals are delivered?
faqs.answer: Your parent must be present to receive the delivery. Meals cannot be left unattended. If they miss a delivery, they'll need to contact their provider to reschedule.

faqs.question: How many meals will my parent actually receive each week?
faqs.answer: It depends on which provider serves their area. St. Mary's Food Bank typically provides one meal per week, while YWCA in Glendale provides up to seven meals weekly. The Area Agency on Aging will tell you which provider your parent qualifies for.

faqs.question: Does my parent's house or savings account affect eligibility?
faqs.answer: No asset limits are specified in Arizona's program—only income limits apply. Your parent can own their home and have savings and still qualify if their monthly income is below the limit.

faqs.question: What if we live in a small town—is this program available everywhere in Arizona?
faqs.answer: The program is statewide, but different providers serve different areas. Call the Area Agency on Aging at 602-264-4357 to find out which provider serves your parent's specific location.
